content.problemStatement:
The Physics teacher in Tanu's class is teaching concepts of a bouncing ball. The rubber ball that she is using has the property that if the ball is dropped from height H then, it bounces back to maximum height H/F. So after first bounce it rises up to maximum height H/F, after second bounce to maximum height H/F2, after third bounce to maximum height H/F3, and so on.

The class has **N** children, and the teacher wants to select two children such that when the taller child drops the ball from his height, ball reaches a maximum height equal to the height of the shorter child after some (possibly zero) number of bounces. Note that zero bounces mean heights of the two children will be same. Given the heights of the children in the class **Height[i]**, can you tell how many ways are there for the teacher to select two children for the demonstration? **Note** that when heights are same, the pair is only counted once (See first example for further clarification).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of test cases T. Then, for each test case, the first line contains two space-separated integers N and F, where N is the number of children and F is the factor by which the ball bounces back. The second line of each test case contains N space-separated integers representing the heights of the children.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of ways the teacher can select two children for the demonstration.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first case, the three pairs are (child 1, child 2), (child 2, child 3) and (child 1, child 3). For the second case also, the three pairs are (child 1, child 2), (child 2, child 3) and (child 1, child 3).

solution.approach: For each pair of children, we need to check if the height of the taller child can be reduced to the height of the shorter child after some number of bounces. We can do this by dividing the height of the taller child by the bounce factor F until we get a height less than or equal to the height of the shorter child. If we can find such a number of bounces, we count this pair as a valid selection.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2 * log(H)) — where H is the maximum height of a child. The reason is that for each test case, we iterate over all pairs of children (O(N^2)), and for each pair, we divide the height of the taller child by F until we get a height less than or equal to the height of the shorter child (O(log(H))).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — we only use a constant amount of space to store the input and the count of valid selections.

code.Python3:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    N, F = map(int, input().split())
    heights = list(map(int, input().split()))
    count = 0
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            taller = max(heights[i], heights[j])
            shorter = min(heights[i], heights[j])
            bounces = 0
            while taller > shorter:
                taller //= F
                bounces += 1
            if taller == shorter:
                count += 1
    print(count)
